candidates.stmt: As a long-term Aldborough resident, I care about safer streets, cleaner neighbourhoods, better roads, and strong local services. I want to support practical improvements, listen to residents, and help ensure our community remains a great place to live for everyone.

I’ve called Redbridge and Essex home for over 50 years, and this borough has shaped who I am. I'm standing for Redbridge Borough Council because I believe our community deserves councillors who are truly rooted in local life, who put people and nature before profit.

My community involvement began when I joined Redbridge Cycling Club in 2012. As Women's Representative on their committee for six years, I discovered a passion for making cycling accessible to everyone. That led me to projects like Cycle for All, supporting families on low incomes and SEN groups, as well as leading women-only rides in Redbridge.

I support women, people of colour, and members of the LGBTQI+ community to feel seen, empowered, and physically strong through every stage of their lives. I'm especially passionate about helping menopausal women, who are let down by health services due to decades of neglect in medical research.

I spent ten years as a live-in carer for my disabled grandmother, which gave me a deep personal understanding of the challenges faced by those with mobility restrictions. From navigating public transport, our pavements, or local green spaces in a wheelchair. That experience stays with me, and it drives my commitment to a borough that works for everyone.

I spend as much time outdoors as possible, protecting Redbridge's green spaces is close to my heart. If lost, they are almost impossible to reclaim. I have serious concerns about the planned changes to Fairlop Waters and the impact they will have on local wildlife, it is decisions like this that demands we have councillors who will listen to residents and stand firm.

I want to work with you on the issues that matter most: sustainable housing, cleaner transport, protecting our environment, supporting local businesses, and building health services that truly serve our whole community.

Let's build a greener, fairer Redbridge, united together.

I am standing as an independent candidate for Barkingside because our community needs strong local representation, not party politics.

I have lived here nearly 20 years and raised my family locally. After a senior private sector career, I founded a business and served residents through community work.

I will fight crime, overdevelopment, traffic and council failures.

I have lived in Redbridge since I was 16, including 20 years in South Woodford and since 2000 in Barkingside. My children attended local schools and I volunteer with the Air Cadets. I run a training company and IT rental business, volunteer at a food bank, and will fight over development, traffic problems, and give residents a stronger voice.

Vinod Agrawal, a 20-year North Ilford resident intends to improve Barkingside lives particularly infill and overdevelopments without supporting services, traffic congestion near hotspots, addressing safety concerns, help save character of Barking side high street, anti-social behaviour, cleaner streets, through efficient budgeting, enforcement, and community-driven solutions.

I am working as an Accounts Manager for a Logistics company and have lived in Woods Estate for the last 12 years.  I feel our ward is becoming a crime hotspot for car theft and drug dealings. We need more police patrols in our streets to prevent crime than doing the paper work after the crime has happened. If I become a councillor I will promote democracy in Redbridge council and avoid schemes like Quiet Street’s being implemented without public consultation. I will use my financial skills to fight for better allocations of council funds to where it’s actually needed.

I’m 60 years old and I’ve lived in Redbridge for over 40 years. I’ve raised my family here, shopped on our high streets, and seen how our area has changed. I know Bridge ward, and I know what needs fixing.
As your councillor I’ll get Broadmead Road open. I’ll scrutinise every line of council spending to stop waste and overspend, because it’s your money. And I’ll fight to finally reduce our council tax bills. Redbridge residents are paying more and getting less, and that has to change. experienced, plain-speaking, and on your side.

Thomas Jose – Reform UK Candidate for Bridge Ward

Dear Resident,

My name is Thomas Jose, and I am proud to stand as your Reform UK candidate for Bridge Ward. I first made Redbridge my home in 2004, and this community means a lot to me.

I am not a career politician. I have over 20 years of experience in business, education, healthcare, and the UK railway. I understand the real challenges facing local families, workers, and small businesses.

I am standing to bring honest, corruption-free leadership and a common-sense approach to local politics.

My priorities:

✔ Corruption-free leadership✔ Better local services✔ Support for local businesses and jobs✔ Safer neighbourhoods

I believe in working hard, listening to residents, and delivering real results.

Redbridge is my home, and I want to help build a stronger and safer Bridge Ward for everyone.

Vote Reform UK. Vote Thomas Jose.

I have lived in Redbridge for 55 years and reside within the Bridge Ward. My wife and I raised two children here and I served as a magistrate for over a decade. As such I know the harsh impact of crime in our neighbourhood.

I believe Reform UK will work hard to reduce crime, build houses, repair roads and invest in the locality. I stand for local issues.

Trust Reform UK. Vote Vasis.

My name is Joe McIntosh, I am currently working in TV and Broadcasting as an audio visual engineer and am responsible for the successes of some of the largest live events and TV shows, predominantly in the UK but in some cases globally. Aside from this I am also a highly productive and highly skilled multimedia artist working in graphic design, photography, music and video. I like to see the bigger picture and I have a real passion for learning and gaining different creative skillsets.

I have lived in Bridge ward for the last 3 years, moving from the Midlands to work within the audio visual industry. Prior to this I had worked throughout the entirety of the UK’s National Testing Programme in the fight against Covid-19. It is here where I witnessed the complete incompetence of the Conservative Government and their inability to effectively and efficiently role out the appropriate measures to fight off the pandemic and support the public. It is also here where I witnessed how corrupt the whole testing programme was and how much of the taxpayers money was being well and truly wasted and sent to line the pockets of the Tory Government’s wealthy benefactors.

For most of my life I have worked on many different creative projects, primarily in music. Over recent years my artistic output has been heavily focused on covering more political themes such as capitalist exploitation, socio-political anxiety and general economic frustration. As an artist I have always strived to create work that critiques and questions the society we live in. I always wanted to make a difference to better the world and decided that getting more directly involved in politics might offer me the opportunity to help lead real change for the country and the community.

I had always thought politics wasn’t a place for people like me, a real working person, and was just a place for those of privilege. The Green Party gave me hope that we can fill Government positions with real people who can make real de

I have been either a resident or closely associated with Havering and Redbridge most of my early and adult life. I was born in Redbridge, grew up in the borough, have family who have lived here all their lives, and worked throughout both areas. More than that, I am also passionate and genuinely motivated by making sure that outer east London gets the services, funding, and recognition it deserves. As director of Eastside Community Heritage, I've secured £1,500,000+ funding for community led projects in east London; it is vital that we continue to build social capital in our communities.

We are a changing community and an asset to London; our schools and children’s services demonstrate this and we need to make sure that it is recognised across Greater London. I am a candidate who believes in equality and I understand that the focus too often remains on central London leaving outer London boroughs feeling ignored. I can make a difference because I know the issues, concerns, and - most importantly - the residents of Havering and Redbridge.

My political career has been shaped by my campaigning activism on the doorstep, the party positions I have held, and my two terms as a Councillor in Newham and currently as Labour Party Group Chair and Councillor in Redbridge. I am an active street and doorstep campaigner; having also been sub agent for a number of elections. I understand the importance of effective organising. Nowhere was this more apparent than in my Barkingside campaign last year, which we successfully secured as a labour win for the first time in its 50 year history. I have also served a number of party positions, including West Ham CLP women’s officer (1993-1996). As Labour Group chair I am fortunate enough to preside over the largest Labour group in Redbridge history, with many new members. This has required some innovation and development in terms of building a collegiate culture of working.

*I'm passionate about making politics for the benefit of residents, not political parties.*
I've lived in Churchfields for the last 20 years. My children attended Churchfields and Woodbridge schools and my granddaughter is at Oakdale. As a nutritionist working with schools, I supported the health and wellbeing of families living in the community and, while a school governor, I was responsible for monitoring school food standards. I now advise several local community gardening projects.

As a member of *Faiza Shaheen’s* 2024 General Election campaign team I became passionate about doing politics differently - community-led, _not party-led._ 
I am one of *three Chingford & Woodford Green Community Independents* standing in Churchfields, alongside *Rita Mahli and Helen Watson*.

As a group we have pledged that, if elected, we will:
1. *Push for the repair and re-opening of Broadmead Road bridge*. It's continued closure costs residents time and money, as well as bringing a host of problems including road rage, poor air quality and more potholes! We pledge to prioritise getting this vital local artery open again. If a Labour council, under a Labour mayor and a Labour government can’t find a way to fix it, what use are they?
2. *Improve local facilities for young and old alike.* Redbridge council has ignored residents when there have been opportunities to create community facilities locally, such as on the old Pavilion site in *Elmhurst Gardens*. We will work with local community groups to progress plans for sites like these and campaign for funding for them to become a reality.
3. *Hold to account those running failing services for children and adults in Redbridge.* OFSTED say the borough’s services for children and young people with special educational needs and/or disabilities ‘need urgent reform’. The Care and Quality Commission (CQC) called adult social care provision in Redbridge ‘inadequate’. We pledge to get those running these services to answer directly to our c

*I believe that voting Independent in the local elections on 7th May can encourage grass-roots democracy and engagement with local communities.*

I have lived in Churchfields for 25 years and both my children attended Churchfields and Woodbridge schools. I work for a trade union and, until national Labour removed Faiza Shaheen as its candidate in 2024, I had been chair of the local Labour Party branch for ten years. I now chair Chingford & Woodford Green Community Independents, a group committed to community-led politics that benefits residents, not political parties. Among other issues, I‘ve campaigned for progress on the new hospital desperately needed at Whipps Cross. I sit on the board of trustees of two charities - The Friends of Redbridge Music School, which supports the music education of 11,000 children in the borough, and Show Racism the Red Card, a national charity that educates against racism in schools and workplaces. 
I am one of *three Chingford & Woodford Green Community Independents* standing in Churchfields, alongside *Rita Mahli and Jillian Ghose*.

*As a group we have pledged that, if elected, we will:*
1. *Push for the repair and re-opening of Broadmead Road bridge*. It's continued closure costs residents time and money, as well as bringing a host of problems including road rage, poor air quality and more potholes! We pledge to prioritise getting this vital local artery open again. If a Labour council, under a Labour mayor and a Labour government can’t find a way to fix it, what use are they?
2. *Improve local facilities for young and old alike.* Redbridge council has ignored residents when there have been opportunities to create community facilities locally, such as on the old Pavilion site in *Elmhurst Gardens*. We will work with local community groups to progress plans for sites like these and campaign for funding for them to become a reality.
3. *Hold to account those running failing services for children and adults in Redbridge.* OFSTED say

*I believe Churchfields residents deserve better representation.*

I have lived in Churchfields for 14 years. I am university worker and trade unionist, representing members and handling casework.

I am standing because I believe Churchfields residents deserve better representation. I was inspired by Faiza Shaheen’s 2024 general election campaign, which demonstrated the power of progressive community-led politics. Since the last general election, I have been supporting local campaigns in Redbridge and working closely with former independent Councillor Rosa Gomez. I am one of three Chingford & Woodford Green Community Independents standing in Churchfields, alongside Jillian Ghose and Helen Watson.

Our Pledges

Vote for us in the local elections on Thursday 7 May and we pledge to:
1	Push for the repair and re-opening of Broadmead Bridge. The continued closure costs residents time and money, as well as bringing a host of problems including road rage, poor air quality and more potholes! We pledge to prioritise getting this vital local artery open again. If a Labour council, under a Labour mayor and a Labour government can’t find a way to fix it, what use are they?
2	Improve local facilities for young and old alike. Redbridge council has ignored residents when there have been opportunities to create community facilities locally, such as on the old Pavilion site in Elmhurst Gardens. We will work with local community groups to progress plans for sites like these and campaign for funding for them to become a reality.
3	Hold to account those running failing services for children and adults in Redbridge. OFSTED say the borough’s services for children and young people with special educational needs and/or disabilities ‘need urgent reform’. The Care and Quality Commission (CQC) called adult social care provision in Redbridge ‘inadequate’. We pledge to get those running these services to answer directly to our community and push for the changes need to improve them.
4	Call out

I have proudly lived in Redbridge for 29 years and care deeply about our community. I understand the challenges we face - from congestion to safety - and I am committed to working hard to deliver practical solutions and a better future for Clayhall residents.

As a local resident, landlord, and businessman, I understand the real pressures people in our community face, rising costs, crime, and a council that too often fails to deliver value for money. I’m standing to bring practical, results-driven leadership to our area.

My priority is to make our neighbourhood safer and better managed. That means pushing for tougher action on anti-social behaviour, drug dealing, vehicle theft, and fly tipping, issues residents raise time and time again but see little improvement on.

I will also stand up for local businesses and high streets, ensuring fair parking solutions so shops can thrive, not suffer because of poor planning and lack of enforcement.

On housing, I support improving standards and making better use of existing homes, while protecting green spaces and keeping our area a place people are proud to live in. I believe in responsible development, not overbuilding, but smart, community-focused growth.

I will challenge wasteful spending and fight for better value from the council, so your money goes further,  whether that’s cleaner streets, better-maintained parks, or stronger local services.

Most importantly, I will be visible, approachable, and accountable listening to residents and acting on what matters, not just making promises.

This is about common sense, local focus, and getting things done properly.

I’ve lived in Redbridge for almost 36 years and have been actively involved in community and voluntary work.

With a background in public health and MedTech consultancy, I understand the importance of strong services.

My priorities are supporting young people, improving healthcare access, and ensuring safe streets and public spaces.

"I am really looking forward to standing as an independent candidate and serving our community.  Cranbrook deserves a council that listens, acts, and delivers.

This local election is about the issues that affect our daily lives — safer streets, cleaner neighbourhoods, reliable services, and real support for families and local businesses. Too often, residents feel overlooked or unheard. That needs to change.

I am committed to being a strong, visible voice for Cranbrook within Redbridge Council — someone who will listen to your concerns, stand up for our community, and work tirelessly to get things done. From tackling fly-tipping and improving road safety, to protecting green spaces and supporting local high streets, my focus is on practical solutions that make a real difference. This is our community, and together we can make it better.

On election day, vote for positive change. Vote for a councillor who will put Cranbrook first.

Mohammed Asif

I’m Richard, the son of a British army officer.  I grew up on army bases in the UK & Germany during the Cold War, under constant threat of Soviet invasion or nuclear annihilation and understand how important our armed forces, secure borders, NATO & our special relationship with USA is to our fundamental freedoms and democracy.
I spent a career in IT & investment banking working in UK and around the world and understand the need for balanced budgets and reducing the tax burden on hard working people and business so they can thrive, because without their taxes we cannot pay for the services and benefits we also need.
My wife and I adopted our daughter from Delhi and we have lived in Redbridge for 25 years in the family home purchased by my wife’s parents who came here legally from India in the 1950s.  In 2017 I qualified as a Physics teacher (QTS) after working for 1.5 years at Ilford County High grammar school.
There is plenty of money at Town Hall but it is not being proritised on local people.  Council resources must be focused on local residents not wasted on pet projects, outside groups and ideological national policies.  I support lower council taxes and balanced budgets not excessive salaries for Council executives.
I will work to make life better for you and your family by cleaning up your streets, fixing broken pavements & pot holes, clearing flying tipping, making you feel safe in your home and on your streets, and cutting waste wherever I see it.  I believe in zero tolerance for anti social behavior, burglary, shop lifting, hooliganism & violence, and crimes such as drug dealing, sexual assault and open prostitution much of it controlled by criminal gangs. 
Redbridge is broken and we need reform to start making things better again and give hope back to our families, community, and nation.

A passionate volunteer with a professional background in farming and teaching, I am committed to building a safer, stronger Fairlop. I will tackle youth safety concerns, drug dealing, vehicle theft, and fly tipping, while protecting green spaces and improving social housing, schools, and parks. I am dedicated to supporting senior citizens and helping residents manage the cost of living through practical, trusted local solutions.

I'm standing to put our community first. Our team is focused on safer streets, reliable bus and tube services, and making sure residents are heard. We'll tackle anti-social behaviour around high streets and transport hubs - and we're already out litter picking to keep Fairlop clean.

I’ve lived in Redbridge since 2020, raising four young children here. I see waste collection issues, streets that don’t feel safe, and tenants struggling for basic repairs and respect. I see a healthcare system that erects barriers rather than opening doors.

I’ve spent 25 years working in health services—most of that time helping low and middle-income countries bridge gaps in care. That work taught me that progress is slow, hard, and only possible when communities lead it through community clinics. The same is true here.

But change doesn’t come from one person. It comes from us—together. As a co-op governor, I worked alongside parents and staff to lift a local school from Requiring Improvement to Outstanding.

I’m not here to be the answer. I’m here to help us build it. That’s my promise to Redbridge.

What i Stand for:

I am standing for the Green Party because we refuse to accept how Redbridge is being run.

Too few council homes. Tenants’ rights forgotten. Fly-tipping everywhere, but enforcement is weak and uncreative. The recycling centre is difficult to access. Green spaces are disappearing, even as housing options for our communities shrink. And residents have no real say in how their own money is spent.

These aren’t small problems. They are signs of a leadership that has stopped listening.

I have raised these issues again and again—at meetings, in letters, with neighbours and councillors. I’ve spoken up and shown up. But time after time, I’ve watched them be ignored or brushed aside.

That frustration didn’t break me. It moved me.

I’m not standing because I want a title or a political career. I’m standing because I want a different way. A way where we listen, act, and build together—on housing that is genuinely affordable, on waste systems that actually work, on protecting our green spaces, and on putting budget decisions back where they belong: in the hands of residents.

No more empty promises. No more being brushed aside. Together, we can build a Redb

Dear residents,
My name is Amir Ijaz Sandhu, and I am proud to be standing as the Green Party candidate for Fullwell ward.

I am a solicitor based in Redbridge, and I have dedicated my professional life to the principles of justice, fairness, peace, and human rights.

I am standing because I believe Fullwell deserves a councillor who will truly listen to residents and stand up for our community.

As someone who lives and works in Redbridge, I understand the real challenges local families face:

- Rising living costs
- Pressure on housing and rents
- Cleaner and safer streets
- Protecting parks and green spaces
- Better local services
- Stronger community safety

I have always been passionate about promoting justice, peace, and human dignity, both locally and globally.

At a time when many people feel let down by the established parties, Green Party offers a principled and progressive alternative.

I am asking for your support so that Fullwell has a strong local voice at Redbridge Council.

On 7 May, vote Green. Vote for Amir Ijaz Sandhu.

Hi, I’m Shorful. I’ve lived in Goodmayes for 16 years with my family and am deeply connected to our community. I run a data consultancy and previously worked in senior roles across technology, media and the NHS after completing my PhD in Psychology. I have also served as Chairman of 7th Goodmayes Scouts and support several charities. My priorities are improving public safety, strengthening health services and tackling street litter and fly-tipping to keep Goodmayes clean, safe and thriving.

My name is Amjad Iqbal (Rana Amjad), and I am proud to stand as a candidate committed to serving our local community. With over 15 years of business experience and 12 years running my business in Goodmayes, opposite the station, I have built strong relationships with local residents and gained a deep understanding of the issues that matter most to our area.

As a local business owner, I see every day the challenges facing families, young people, and small businesses. I am passionate about listening to residents, representing their concerns, and working hard to bring positive, practical change to our community.

I strongly believe in the Green Party’s values of:
	•	Fairness for all
	•	Sustainable local development
	•	Community-led decision-making

If elected, I will work to ensure that local voices are heard and that Redbridge becomes a greener, fairer, and more inclusive place for everyone.

Thank you for your support.

Redbridge resident of 12 years, I’m standing to protect Hainault’s green spaces, including Brocket Way, tackle crime and cleanliness, and address concerns near Manford Way.

I will ensure residents, not top-down decisions, shape our ward’s future.

With over 20 years’ experience, I provide strong, accountable leadership and deliver the results we need.

I'm a father of two and been a Redbridge resident for 12 years, I seek election as a Conservative councillor for Hainault to deliver safer streets through visible policing and targeted action. 
Having personally experienced burglary and repeated car break-ins, I understand the frustration caused by inadequate policing. I will prioritise action against car theft, break-ins, and burglary so others do not face what me & my family went through.

I will also commit to hold monthly surgeries to listen to residents’ concerns (such as support to elderly residents, youth crime, ASB etc) and work together to devise a transparent, community-focused solutions that strengthen and unite our local neighbourhood.

Hi, I’m Ruthba. I’m a mother of two and a long-standing Redbridge resident who cares deeply about our community. Through my work in education and frontline public service. I have supported families with housing, welfare and essential services. I founded the Clean Up Ilford Lane campaign and have worked with residents to protect local services. My priorities are easing cost-of-living pressures, safer and cleaner streets, and fair access to housing and local services.

I’m Iqbal. I have lived in Redbridge for over 22 years and worked for central government as a civil servant for two decades, while also running a small business. I understand the everyday pressures residents face with housing, services and local regulations. My priorities are affordable housing, tackling crime and anti-social behaviour, supporting youth centres, and improving the parking permit system. I believe residents deserve
practical, common-sense representation that holds the council accountable.

I’m Ramona. I have lived in Ilford for 15 years and worked at Loxford Primary School for 14 years, building strong relationships with families, pupils and staff. As a cover teacher and local parent, I understand the challenges residents face. My priorities are supporting schools and young people, protecting vital local services, and ensuring our streets and public spaces are safe, clean and welcoming. I am committed to listening to residents and serving our community with integrity and care. As an east European living in this community, I have noticed that many people struggle to connect with their local council representatives. I want to help bridge that gap and make sure that those who feel unheard have someone willing to speak up for them.

I am the only independent candidate at this election, giving residents a real choice to vote for someone committed to them. 

Residents in Mayfield have a great opportunity to break free from the domination of Labour and Conservatives who, for decades, have neglected local people whilst imposing huge tax rises. Locally, our streets are unsafe, unclean, and services are crumbling. I'm in politics to change this and to serve residents with integrity, unlike our current Ilford South MP and former Mayfield ward councillor, Jas Athwal, who as Council leader failed to licence his own rental flats, whilst demanding everyone else did.

Over the past 10 years, this Labour Council has had a very shoddy record in delivering for the people of Redbridge. Our streets are filthy and crime is at epidemic levels. We desperately need local people to control their own destiny. I live locally and work for the NHS. I'm also a magistrate, and I came a very close second when I stood to be the local MP at the last General Election. 

If elected, I will fight hard for Mayfield residents at the Town Hall and deliver for them at this time of great uncertainty. People are worried about the cost of living, the state of our neighbourhoods, the huge increases in our Council Tax and the chronically poor state of our housing.

With a few exceptions, most Cllrs in Redbridge are exclusively Labour, so I'm asking residents to lend me their vote and give me the opportunity to show what an Ilford Independents candidate can achieve for local residents. As their Councillor, local people and their needs will be my priority.

I lived in Redbridge since 2014. I love this part of London because it is full of different people and has a long history of residents standing up for what is fair. I speak five languages—including Farsi—which helps me listen to and talk with all kinds of neighbours in our community.
I’ve always wanted my work to be about helping others. I have a masters degree in social and political science and I have spent my career working within the charity sector. For the last ten years, I’ve worked for charities that support people with mental health issues. This job has shown me that when people go through a hard time, the government often isn't there to catch them—even though we all pay our taxes to make sure those services exist.
If you vote for me in Mayfield, I will work on three main things:
• More Help for Everyone: Redbridge is like a ‘charity desert.’ Compared to
other parts of London, we don't have enough local groups to help people with things like mental health. I want to change that so no one has to struggle alone.
• Cleaner Air: Mayfield is near some of the busiest, smoggiest roads in London. This isn't healthy for our lungs, especially for children. I will fight for cleaner air and safer streets for everyone.
• Cleaning Up Our Streets: I’m tired of seeing litter and dirty pavements. We pay our taxes for clean streets, and I want to make sure that money is actually spent on making Mayfield look cared for.
I’m not a professional politician. I’m a charity worker and a neighbour who believes we deserve better. On Thursday, May 7th, a vote for me is a vote for a fairer, cleaner, and more helpful Mayfield.

Hornchurch and Upminster is a community where my family, friends, and neighbours live. I envision a thriving Hornchurch and Upminster, characterised by vibrant high streets filled with unique, family-run boutique businesses. Ensuring safety for everyone, whether you're a business owner or a student coming home from school, is a priority for me.

Our local NHS trust has faced significant challenges under the current government. I am committed to seeing it, along with all our hospitals, flourish once again through strong leadership and necessary investments.   As your MP, I will listen to all residents, understanding their concerns and ideas for improving our area. I will be your voice in Westminster, advocating for meaningful change.   Please get in touch with your questions and suggestions.

I have lived in Redbridge since 2009 and in Newbury since 2017. As a local father and IT professional with 15 years of problem-solving experience, I am standing to ensure our ward gets the attention it deserves.

My priorities for Newbury:
Safer Streets: Tackling crime and improving road safety.
Cleaner Neighbourhoods: Fixing fly-tipping and maintaining our local environment.
Better Services: Demanding transparency and accountability in how your council tax is spent.

I am not a career politician—I am a local resident who wants to listen to you and deliver practical solutions for our community.

Hi, I’m Imran. I’ve lived in Redbridge for 25 years and have been a doctor for 20 years, worked locally as a GP for 7 years, supporting patients through local hospitals, GP surgeries and out-of-hours services. I’m passionate about improving healthcare, ensuring safe streets and reviewing overdevelopment in our area. I want to work with residents to build a healthier, safer and stronger community for everyone.

Hi, I’m Rayhana. I’ve lived and worked in Redbridge for 11 years as a local GP, caring for residents and understanding the challenges families face. As a mother with children in local schools, I care deeply about our community and its future. My priorities are affordable housing, protecting vital local services and ensuring our streets and public spaces are safe and welcoming. I want to bring compassion, practical experience and a strong community voice to represent residents and help build a healthier, safer and more supportive neighbourhood.

Hi, I’m Kes. I’ve lived in Seven Kings since 2009 and have worked in the NHS while also serving as a foster carer. These experiences have given me a strong understanding of the pressures facing local families. My priorities are tackling overdevelopment, improving street cleanliness and safety, and investing in youth services and opportunities. I believe Seven Kings deserves strong local representation that listens to residents and focuses on practical solutions that improve everyday life.

Everything I do is driven by a desire to help people. I was a physics teacher until 2024, and I loved working with young people. I left the profession after seeing the lasting impact of Covid-19 on young people’s education, and feeling frustrated trying to close the attainment gap with consistent underfunding from the government.

I now work at a charity focusing on sexual violence prevention through education, visiting schools across the country and training teachers. I have previously led improvements to gender-inclusive teaching, as I firmly believe education should be accessible to all, no matter their identity or socioeconomic background. I am not afraid to challenge structures and systems to push for meaningful change.

I grew up in East London and have lived in Redbridge with my family for the last 16 years. This is my home. I see my friends, family and neighbours struggling with the cost of living, cuts to public services and poor transport links. I want to do something about this, because we all deserve better.

I’m a bit of a maths nerd and have taught enough maths to know that without measurement, maths tells you nothing about the world. Too much economic debate now skips that step, hiding behind clever-sounding equations. What we get sounds serious, but doesn’t connect to the real economy and everyday people. As an educator my priority on Redbridge Council will be to remove the things getting in the way of you taking part in decisions. Not just because it’s good democracy, but because it’s good economics too.

Scott Wilding is a Wanstead resident for over 35 years having grown up in Wanstead Village, attending Wanstead Church School and Wanstead High School.  Scott has been the chair of the Wanstead Society since 2009 and a trustee of the Wanstead Playground Association since 2014 helping to raise funds to renovate this key community asset.  A dedicated community activist Scott helped establish the bi-monthly litter picks around Wanstead in 2016, Addison Road Street parties in 2012 and is a keen community gardener.  A public sector worker, Scott spent 12 years working for Redbridge Council and is ready to use his wealth of experience to protect and promote our community.

Born and raised in Ilford, I went to Christchurch Primary and Valentines High School, and still live next to the park here in Valentines Ward. I’ve been leading Council scrutiny of SEND, securing a new school for our ward. I’m determined that not a single child is left behind in our education system.

A vote for me, Khayer and Kumud is a vote to:
1) Crack down on crime, poor housing & the cost-of-living
2) Invest in Valentines Park & take action on the nature emergency
3) Get more school choice, more school places & better youth facilities
4) Protect & upgrade your bin collection & improve recycling

Hi, I’m Marwan. I’ve lived in Valentines Ward for 15 years and have spent much of my life working locally in education supporting young people. I’m a university lecturer and former secondary school teacher. I volunteer and coach football locally at Frenford and am a regular at Valentines Parkrun. I care deeply about our ward and neighbours. My priorities are safer streets and protecting our parks.

The son of immigrant parents from Bangladesh, I grew up in the East End, before moving to Gants Hill 15 years ago. I’ve made it my mission to make Ilford safer. I’ve secured 25 extra police officers for Ilford Town Centre and our ward, & doubled local CCTV cameras.

A vote for me, Alex and Kumud is a vote to:
1) Crack down on crime, poor housing & the cost-of-living
2) Invest in Valentines Park & take action on the nature emergency
3) Get more school choice, more school places & better youth facilities
4) Protect & upgrade your bin collection & improve recycling

I moved to Ilford some 30 years ago. Over those years I’ve volunteered for various local groups, plus my trade union, Unison, and I’ve been building community to tackle social exclusion. I’m focussed on ensuring the Council delivers the services most important to you.

A vote for me, Alex and Khayer is a vote to:
1) Crack down on crime, poor housing & the cost-of-living
2) Invest in Valentines Park & take action on the nature emergency
3) Get more school choice, more school places & better youth facilities
4) Protect & upgrade your bin collection & improve recycling

I'm Brian, a lifelong Redbridgian. Councillors should be accessible. In Valentines Ward, surgeries are by appointment only. I will hold regular, open, drop-in sessions so you can speak to me directly. I will work to make the council fair, transparent and impartial, and help balance the budget and reduce debt.

As a local resident who lives in the ward, I’ve decided to put my name forward.

Together, we can make a difference in our community. Wanstead Park Ward is where my husband and I are raising our family. I was born locally in Whipps Cross Hospital. We live on the Warren Estate and our children attend Aldersbrook Primary School.

As a local mother of two, I have been very active on issues relating to local school admissions, supporting local families to understand and navigate the process. I also led the successful local campaign to prevent an out of character phone mast being built in front of St Mary’s Church. I could do so much more if you elect me as your councillor.

Wanstead Park is my home, and we are fortunate to live in such a beautiful area. I’ll fight to protect and conserve our superb local green spaces, support our local NHS, fight for better bus services and Central Line services, fight for more police and action on crime, and ensure we have the very best local facilities and services here in Wanstead Park.

It would be the privilege of my life to represent our corner of Redbridge as your local councillor. I will never take your vote for granted, and I’ll always fight for our area.

I care deeply about this community and believe local politics should be rooted in lived experience, not career ambition.

I have lived in Aldersbrook since 2019, moving here from Wood Street with my family, including 2 young daughters.

My decision to stand is shaped by my own experiences navigating public services and advocating for change. I have lived experience of maternal mental health challenges, which made me acutely aware of how difficult it can be to access timely and appropriate care.

I established a free support community to help mothers and birthing people navigate the healthcare system and access the support they need. This work has shown me how powerful community led solutions can be, particularly when led by people with lived experience, but also how urgently the systems need to improve so that no one falls through the gaps.

I worked for thirty years as a secondary school teacher in Redbridge. More recently I have been working with organisations that promote creativity and wellbeing through music. I’ve been working with older people, and people with a range of mental health challenges. Last year, one of the groups I work with won the Pride of Waltham Forest Cultural Connections award. Working with such a diverse range of people and facilitating their expression and fulfilment makes me feel extremely privileged and proud.

I could list many words that reflect my values. But education, creativity, care, compassion, and community are ones that come to mind right now.

I am standing for the Green Party because we oppose the policies of austerity that have had such a devastating effect on our public services. In education, for example, teachers and students face overwhelming challenges because of the negative impacts of funding cuts. With greater investment in our schools, healthcare, and essential services, we can begin to reverse the impacts of those cuts and create a fairer society in which everyone can thrive.

At a local level, The Green Party’s commitment to listening, engaging, and acting on the concerns of local people will help to make a positive change. With the guidance and support of the local community, I want to be able to work towards that change.

I believe that we don’t have to accept things as they are. We can hope for communities and a society in which our needs are met, so we can live happy, fulfilled lives.

I live locally with my husband and two young children and I am an active member of the community - leading local campaigns to improve play provision and green spaces. I have also raised thousands of pounds for my daughters’ school and set up a local children’s nature club. 
I understand and share many of the concerns we’ve been hearing on the doorstep about rising crime, road safety, pollution, the housing crisis, worsening inequality, Brexit and the impact of cuts imposed by the Tory government. 
Along with fellow Labour candidates, Paul Donovan and Daniel Morgan-Thomas, I’ve been campaigning on issues like reversing cuts to the policing budget, reinstating the old W12 bus timetable, and introducing 20mph speed limits on residential roads. 
My professional career has been spent campaigning at a national, European and international level for children’s rights, the environment and for the Labour Party. I would like to bring this knowledge of how to affect change to my role as a councillor, championing the needs of children, the environment and the local services that our communities rely on, and generally supporting all of our residents. 
I hope I will have the opportunity to represent Wanstead Village as a councillor and work with local residents to make a difference.

I am a former health journalist now working as a health regulator adviser.

I live in Wanstead Park with my husband and two grown-up daughters who went to
Nightingale, Aldersbrook and Wanstead High Schools.

Before joining the Greens, I was chair of Wanstead Park Labour Branch and left
Labour after many months of frustration and anger over Gaza and austerity with the
final straw being Keir Starmer’s speech on immigration.

I am of Irish descent and grew up in a south London council house where I was the
first in my family to go to university, studying politics at York.

My father was a hospital porter, and I am keen to ensure others benefit from the
same life opportunities I had.

I am an active member of Wanstead Climate Action attending their meetings, hosting
their high street stand and was earlier this month selected to be their spokesperson
for the full council debate where a ‘Nature Emergency’ was declared.
Former Durham student, North East London resident and Labour activist, occasional genealogist and Archers fan.

I live in Wanstead and am a parent at Snaresbrook Primary School. Over the past five years I have focused on raising my daughter, so much of my time is spent locally, especially at places like Christchurch Green after school and the library during the week. It has given me a real understanding of day-to-day life in the area.

I work as a tax adviser and accountant. I previously worked in the City of London in financial services as a tax specialist and auditor, including during the financial crisis, and later ran my own local accountancy practice supporting small businesses and individuals.

I have also been involved in community roles, including serving as Vice-Chair of the Little Ilford Safer Neighbourhood Ward Panel and as Treasurer of the Highgate Society, working with residents and organisations on local issues.

I am standing because I care about Wanstead and want to play a practical role in looking after it.

WHAT I STAND FOR

For me, it starts with protecting what makes Wanstead Village special.

The village feel matters. It’s what makes people want to live here and stay here, and it needs to be protected as the area changes.

Safer walking routes are a priority. Whether it’s children walking to school or families heading to the park, people should feel comfortable and safe. At the moment, speeding and poor road design make that harder than it should be.

We also need a stronger local economy, starting with Wanstead High Street. That means supporting independent businesses and making sure the high street remains somewhere people want to spend time, not just pass through.

At the same time, we need to take more pride in our local environment. Litter, anti-social behaviour and poorly maintained spaces affect how people feel about where they live.

Wanstead is a great place to live. I want to help protect what’s good about it while making practical improvements people will notice day to day.

Kabir

I am a mother to 3 young children, and I am building a creative, inclusive community for the young people of Redbridge. Our workshops promote the “kids teach kids” model, giving children the opportunity to enjoy a variety of creative activities while making empowering connections. Part of my profits are regularly donated to the Redbridge food bank to help them in the fantastic work they do for the community.

I have worked for small businesses, supporting them in their operations in various capacities. I have also worked for a short period of time in the banking sector. I currently serve as a School Governor at a prestigious local grammar school and recently supported a community project that offers digital literacy provisions to residents, especially from disadvantaged backgrounds.

I have lived in Redbridge for more than 25 years, and I thoroughly understand residents’ struggles, whether that is delayed appointments, long waiting times or rising cost of living.

I stand for public consultations to be held for every important decision surrounding the community, where we build trust between the residents and the council, whether that includes the SEND provisions or more support for small local businesses.

We need a peaceful and united Wanstead Village in these divisive times. I am here to facilitate that with more accountability and transparency. We need better engagement between residents and local councillors to achieve the best for everyone.

For me, cohesion and integration are the heart of any thriving community, and since coming back from a career break after the birth of my son, I want work around multiple avenues that entirely benefit the community and open opportunities for all residents.

I have been fortunate enough to make a meaningful contribution to the local community, which has enabled me to understand where the gaps are and how we can fulfil those gaps. I am keen to actively support Wanstead Village, hence I have chosen to stand as the local counci
